---
title: "Volatility Breakout Final Project"
jupyter: python3
---

::: {.hero-banner}
## Developing and Backtesting a Volatility Breakout Strategy

This project studies a volatility and channel breakout strategy for liquid ETFs. It is organized as a Quarto website backed by an `analysis/` Python package, a `shinybroker` data pipeline, and a supervised-learning extension that uses implied-volatility features from Interactive Brokers.
:::

```{python}
from analysis.breakout_strategy import run_analysis

analysis = run_analysis()
overview = analysis["overview"]
metrics = analysis["metrics_display"]
overview
```

## Project Scope

- Build a transparent breakout strategy with explicit entry and exit rules.
- Backtest the strategy on historical price data using a repeatable Python workflow.
- Log every trade so entries, exits, and holding periods are auditable.
- Extend the baseline strategy with a supervised learning position-scaling overlay for breakout quality.

## Strategy Snapshot

The final baseline is a tuned `QQQ` channel-breakout strategy with explicit stop-loss, trailing-stop, timeout, and breakout-failure exits. On top of that baseline, the project adds a compact `QQQ`-only logistic model that uses breakout quality plus options-implied-volatility features to scale position size up or down. In the current out-of-sample comparison, the ML overlay improves both total return and Sharpe ratio, with a slightly worse max drawdown.

## What To Review

- [Asset Selection](asset-selection.qmd) explains the ETF universe and why `QQQ` became the showcase asset.
- [Methodology](methodology.qmd) documents the final trading rules, labels, feature set, and parameter choices.
- [Results](results.qmd) summarizes the tuned baseline and the final `aggr4` ML overlay.
- [Trade Log](trade-log.qmd) contains the blotter, ledger preview, and downloadable output files.
- [Monitoring](monitoring.qmd) defines how to judge whether the strategy is still behaving as expected after deployment.
